headline: Amazon announces 2026 FBA fee updates - average $0.08/unit increase effective Q2
summary: Average FBA fulfillment fee increase of ~$0.08 per unit effective April 1, 2026
Inbound placement service fees updated - split shipments now $0.27-$0.40 per unit
Storage fees adjusted for peak season (Oct-Dec) with 15% increase over 2025
soWhat: Pennies become EBITDA problems at scale. For agencies managing 50-100 SKUs per client, this can mean $5-15K in annual margin erosion that nobody notices until retention conversations start. The real issue: most agencies can't explain where profit went. This is why recovery needs systems, not hope.
postAngle: Amazon fees didn't 'go up a lot'... but that's the problem. Small fee increases compound across inventory. If you run an agency and don't have a margin protection layer, you're leaving retention on the table. Comment 'MARGIN' and I'll share the audit checklist.

headline: Walmart WFS expands to 42 fulfillment centers, introduces 2-day delivery nationwide
summary: WFS network expands from 32 to 42 fulfillment centers across US
New 2-day delivery promise for all WFS items nationwide
Storage fees remain competitive vs Amazon - averaging 20% lower
soWhat: Channel diversification just got easier. When marketplace fees rise on one platform, having operational excellence on another becomes margin strategy, not just revenue strategy. The winning agencies already figured this out - they're not just 'selling more places,' they're operating multichannel with systems that reduce friction, not add it.
postAngle: When Amazon fees rise, channel diversification becomes margin strategy. Walmart WFS expansion makes multichannel realistic for mid-market brands. The question: can your ops handle it without heroics? Comment 'MULTICHANNEL' for the 3-step launch framework.

headline: Amazon Vendor Central updates chargeback policies - 60-day dispute window now standard
summary: Dispute window extended from 30 to 60 days for shortage and pricing chargebacks
New automated reconciliation tools in Vendor Central dashboard
Average vendor faces $25K-$75K in annual chargebacks, 40% are disputable
soWhat: 60 days sounds generous until you realize it's also when most vendors lose track. The policy complexity + recovery windows = missed dollars. This is the 'quiet leak' problem. Recovery windows don't solve themselves - they require systems, documentation, and cadence. Most 1P teams are reactive because they don't have visibility.
postAngle: Myth: If it's automated, it's handled. Reality: Recovery windows + policy complexity = missed dollars. Amazon just gave vendors 60 days to dispute chargebacks. The winning teams already have a system. Comment 'AUDIT' for the vendor chargeback checklist.

headline: FedEx announces peak surcharge adjustments - residential delivery fees up 12% starting March
summary: Residential delivery surcharges increase 12% effective March 15, 2026
Peak season (Nov-Dec) additional surcharges remain at 2025 levels
New zone-based pricing for oversized items - up to 18% increase in zones 5-8
soWhat: Shipping is the silent margin killer. 12% on residential delivery compounds when you're doing 500+ shipments/month. For agencies managing logistics for clients, this is where you prove value - not by absorbing costs, but by surfacing them, quantifying impact, and helping clients adapt their strategies before it hits profitability.
postAngle: FedEx residential surcharges up 12%. Here's what agencies miss: shipping isn't an ops problem, it's a margin problem. If you're not tracking per-unit delivery costs against client profitability, you're guessing. DM 'SHIPPING' for the logistics cost tracking template.

headline: Amazon updates restricted products list - new compliance requirements for supplements and electronics
summary: Enhanced documentation required for dietary supplements - COA, GMP certificates now mandatory
Electronics categories require additional safety certifications by July 1, 2026
Non-compliance can result in listing suppression and account restrictions
soWhat: Policy changes are where unprepared agencies lose clients fast. It's not about the compliance itself - it's about being proactive vs reactive. The agencies that win are the ones who scan for these updates, brief clients before deadlines, and have workflows to handle documentation. This is execution cadence, not just relationship management.
postAngle: When policy/fee changes hit, winning teams respond fast because they have a system. Amazon's new compliance requirements for supplements and electronics drop in July. Is your agency reactive or systematic? Comment 'SYSTEM' for the weekly operating cadence template.

headline: Study: 68% of Amazon sellers don't track true per-unit profitability after all fees
summary: Jungle Scout study surveyed 2,500 sellers - 68% don't calculate total landed cost per unit
Average seller underestimates true costs by 15-20% when accounting for all fees
Top performers track 15+ cost components vs 3-5 for typical sellers
soWhat: This isn't a seller problem - it's an agency opportunity. If 68% of sellers don't know their true costs, and you're the one who can show them, you become indispensable. This is the difference between being a 'growth partner' and being the profit defense layer. Retention increases when you protect margin, not just grow revenue.
postAngle: 68% of sellers don't track true profitability. Here's the agency play: become the one who explains margin, not just drives growth. Your client doesn't want more dashboards - they want more profit and fewer surprises. Comment 'PROFIT' for the 15-component cost tracker.

headline: Walmart Marketplace lowers referral fees for home goods category by 2%
summary: Home goods referral fees reduced from 15% to 13% effective immediately
Part of Walmart's strategy to attract more sellers in competitive categories
WFS fulfillment fees remain unchanged at current rates
soWhat: When one marketplace drops fees while another raises them, it's not just about arbitrage - it's about having the operational capability to take advantage. Can your agency help a client expand to Walmart with the same operational rigor they have on Amazon? Expansion isn't a growth strategy if it breaks ops.
postAngle: Walmart just dropped home goods fees by 2% while Amazon raised FBA fees. Channel expansion isn't about 'selling more places' - it's about operating multichannel without chaos. Can your agency productize expansion? DM 'EXPAND' for the agency multichannel service package template.

headline: Amazon increases IPI threshold to 500 - low IPI sellers face storage limits starting Q3
summary: Inventory Performance Index (IPI) threshold raised from 450 to 500 effective July 1, 2026
Sellers below threshold face storage limits and inbound shipment restrictions
Focus areas: excess inventory, sell-through rate, stranded inventory
soWhat: IPI isn't just a metric - it's a profit indicator. When Amazon forces better inventory management, it's actually helping profitability for those who pay attention. But for agencies, this is where you prove value: helping clients optimize inventory before they hit limits, not after. This is the 'profit defense + operations' layer combined.
postAngle: Amazon raising IPI threshold to 500. Most agencies react when clients hit limits. Winning agencies prevent it. The difference: systematic weekly inventory reviews vs firefighting. This is why execution cadence matters. Comment 'IPI' for the weekly inventory review checklist.
